query: has the spanish flu virus been reconstructed

retrieved_docs.title: H1N1 Influenza (Nursing) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 1918, a deadly influenza pandemic caused by H1N1 influenza virus, also known as the Spanish flu, infected approximately 500 million people around the world and resulted in the deaths of 50 to 100 million people (3% to 5% of the world population) worldwide, distinguishing it as one of the most deadly pandemics in human history. In 2009, a new strain H1N1 swine flu spread fast around the world among humans, and the World Health Organization (WHO) labeled it a pandemic. However, the 2009 H1N1 virus was not zoonotic swine flu because it was not transferred from pigs to humans. Instead, it spread through airborne droplets from human to human, and potentially, through human contact with inanimate objects contaminated with the virus and transferred to the eyes or nose. This virus caused similar symptoms to those seen in swine, possibly due to reassortment of the viral RNA structure, which allowed human-to-human transfer. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: H1N1 Influenza (Nursing) -- Causes
retrieved_docs.content: The H1N1 swine influenza viruses can potentially cause infections in humans if antigenic characteristics of the virus change. In 2009, the pandemic which started in Mexico with the H1N1 strain displayed a combination of segments of 4 different influenza viruses (quadruple genetic reassortment): pig-origin flu north American avian (comprising 34.4%), bird-origin flu of the human influenza strain (comprising 17.5%), North American swine (comprising 30.6%), and Eurasian swine (comprising 17.5%). Due to this coinfection with influenza virus from diverse animal species, the viruses were able to interact, mutate, and form new strains that had variable immunity. Although it had originated in pigs, it was able to spread from human to human. When the flu spreads from human-to-human, instead of from animals to humans, there can be further mutations, making it harder to treat because people have no natural immunity. [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: H1N1 Influenza -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 1918, the H1N1 influenza virus, commonly known as the Spanish flu, sparked a devastating pandemic that infected roughly 500 million individuals worldwide and led to the deaths of an estimated 50 to 100 million people, accounting for 3% to 5% of the global population at the time. This made it one of the deadliest pandemics in human history. [7] Similarly, the H1N1 influenza strain in 2009 was classified as a pandemic by the World Health Organization (WHO). [8] The 2009 H1N1 virus spread through airborne droplets from human to human, possibly via fomites contaminated with the virus, and subsequently transferred to the mucosa or upper respiratory tract. [9] Notably, similarities in symptoms of H1N1 in both humans and pigs arose, potentially due to the viral reassortment of preexisting strains. This similarity in symptoms suggested a commonality in viral pathogenesis across multiple hosts, likely facilitated by reassortment, thus enhancing efficient transmission. [4] [10] During the pandemic, a common misconception was that individuals could contract swine flu from consuming pig products such as bacon, ham, and other pork items. However, the virus is isolated to the respiratory system and does not involve plasma, making transmission through food unlikely. [11] This misunderstanding resulted in substantial commercial losses in the food and tourism industries. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: H1N1 Influenza -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Significant shifts in the envelope proteins, known as antigenic shifts, can lead to the emergence of new influenza strains capable of evading the host immune response and facilitating human-to-human transmission in previously susceptible populations. For instance, the 2009 H1N1 pandemic, originating in Mexico, resulted from the combination of multiple strains, including Eurasian avian-like H1N1, avian H1N1, and a previously reassorted lineage consisting of avian H1N1, human H3N2, and swine influenza viruses.

retrieved_docs.title: H1N1 Influenza -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Although the virus can be observed using electron microscopy, histopathological changes are nonspecific and necessitate additional diagnostic methods for confirmation. These methods include virus isolation through culture, serology, molecular detection via polymerase chain reaction (PCR), or immunohistochemistry (see Image. Electron Microscopic View of H1N1 Influenza Virus Particles). [40] Histological changes in H1N1 influenza pneumonia include interstitial edema with possible inflammatory infiltrate, alveolar proteinaceous exudation associated with membrane formation, thrombosis of capillaries, necrosis of the alveolar septae, intra-alveolar hemorrhage, dislocation of desquamated pneumocytes with pyknotic nuclei into the surrounding alveolar spaces, diffuse alveolar damage with infiltration by the lymphocytes and histiocytes into the interstitium. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: H1N1 Influenza (Nursing) -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Swine flu was first isolated from pigs in the 1930s by researchers in the United States and was subsequently recognized by pork producers and veterinarians as a cause of flu infections in pigs worldwide, and for the next 60 years, H1N1 was the predominant swine influenza strain. People who are closely associated with pigs have been known to develop an infection, and pigs have also been infected with human flu from these handlers. In the vast majority of cases, cross-species transmission of the virus had remained confined to the specific area and not caused national or global infections in either pigs or humans. Unfortunately, due to the potential for genetic variation in the swine flu virus, there is always a possibility for cross-species transmission with the influenza viruses to occur. Investigators concluded that the "2009 swine flu" strain, which originated in Mexico, was termed novel H1N1 flu since it was mainly found infecting humans and exhibits 2 main surface antigens, hemagglutinin type 1 and neuraminidase type 1. The 8 RNA strands in novel H1N1 flu have 1 strand from human flu strains, 2 derived from avian (bird) strains, and 5 that were derived from swine strains. During the 2009 pandemic, the Centers for Disease and Control and Prevention (CDC) estimated that there were 43 to 89 million cases of swine flu reported during a 1-year span, with 1799 deaths in 178 countries worldwide. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Animal influenza viruses can cause infections in humans if the antigenic characteristics of the virus change. When this happens, transmission from person to person is usually inefficient. Influenza pandemics like 1918 and 2009 can occur if the transmission from person to person becomes efficient. Avian influenza, or bird flu, is an infectious disease of birds caused by a variety of influenza A viruses, including A(H5N1), A(H5N8), and H7N9 viruses. These viruses are worrisome as they can change to develop the ability for transmissibility from person to person and start a severe pandemic. A good example of animal-origin influenza is the 2009 pandemic influenza, which is an animal influenza virus that likely started in South America in early 2009 and developed the ability to spread from person to person and spread globally. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Avian Influenza -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although rare, limited human-to-human transmission has been documented, raising concerns about the virus's potential to adapt for more efficient spread among humans. [6] Such adaptations could significantly increase the risk of a global pandemic, highlighting the importance of ongoing surveillance and research.

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are four types of influenza viruses, A, B, C, and D. Influenza types A and B cause human infection annually during the epidemic season. Influenza A has several subtypes according to the combination of hemagglutinin (H) and the neuraminidase (N) proteins that are expressed on the surface of the viruses. There are 18 different hemagglutinin subtypes and 11 different neuraminidase subtypes (H1-18 and N1-11). Influenza A viruses can be characterized by the H and N types such as H1N1 and H3N2 (see Image. Electron Microscopic View of H1N1 Influenza Virus Particles). Influenza B viruses are classified into lineages and strains. Influenza B viruses that have circulated in recent influenza seasons belong to one of two lineages, influenza B Yamagata and influenza B Victoria. Influenza viruses have receptors responsible for making them species-specific.

retrieved_docs.title: H1N1 Influenza -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The main area of H1N1 influenza pathogenesis is the upper and lower respiratory tracts. Mild cases usually show a few pathologic changes in the respiratory tract, but severe cases can show clear pathological pneumonia changes. The pathological findings associated with H1N1 influenza include multifocal destruction, potential desquamation of the pseudo-columnar and columnar epithelial cells, and possibly prominent hyperemia and edema in the submucosa. [32] Thrombus formation at the bronchiolar level is also expected. Sometimes, the acute inflammation is severe and is indicated by hemorrhagic tracheobronchitis and desquamative bronchiolitis. This may cause necrosis of the bronchiolar wall. After necrosis occurs, polymorphs and mononuclear cells infiltrate into the affected area.

retrieved_docs.title: Avian Influenza -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Avian influenza viruses have a segmented, single-stranded RNA genome, enabling genetic reassortment, antigenic drift, or both, which enhances their ability to evolve and adapt to new hosts. [9] [10] [11] These viruses naturally circulate in wild aquatic birds, such as ducks, geese, and shorebirds, which often carry the virus asymptomatically and act as reservoirs. These birds facilitate the spread of the virus to domestic poultry through direct contact or via contaminated water and environments. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Avian Influenza -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging studies are essential for assessing disease severity. Chest radiographs frequently reveal bilateral infiltrates consistent with viral pneumonia. In more advanced cases, computed tomography (CT) scans often demonstrate diffuse ground-glass opacities or consolidation, hallmark findings of acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Influenza -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza viruses replicate in the epithelial cell lining of the upper and lower respiratory tracts. The pathology does not differ between natural or experimental infection. The definitive diagnosis of influenza needs serologic, immunologic, and molecular testing through reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for upper or lower respiratory tract specimens. Mild cases show pathological changes in the respiratory tracts, but severe cases show clear evidence of pathologic changes of pneumonia. The tracheobronchial changes due to influenza infection can be summarized as redness and inflammation with mucous and purulent discharge macroscopically, and desquamation and destruction of the pseudostratified epithelium of the trachea and bronchi with only the basal layer remained viable but inflamed microscopically.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 673
retrieved_docs.content: Neuraminidase inhibitors are classified as direct‑acting antivirals, meaning they target viral enzymes essential for replication. Zanamivir is a specific member of the neuraminidase inhibitor class and therefore also functions as a direct‑acting antiviral. By inhibiting the neuraminidase enzyme of the influenza virus, zanamivir may be used to prevent الإنفلونزا (influenza), highlighting its role in both antiviral therapy and prophylaxis against this common respiratory infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 641
retrieved_docs.content: Zanamivir is classified as a member of the neuraminidase inhibitors, a group of direct‑acting antivirals that target the viral neuraminidase enzyme and block influenza virus replication. Because of this mechanism, zanamivir may be used to prevent الإنفلونزا (influenza), highlighting the role of neuraminidase inhibitors in the prophylaxis and management of influenza infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
retrieved_docs.content: Zanamivir is classified as a member of the neuraminidase inhibitors, which are direct‑acting antivirals. As a neuraminidase inhibitor, zanamivir targets the viral neuraminidase enzyme, thereby blocking the release of progeny influenza viruses and inhibiting viral replication. This relationship places zanamivir within the broader group of direct‑acting antiviral agents that act specifically on viral proteins to treat infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 706
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIVIRALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE constitute a broad therapeutic category, and within this category direct acting antivirals are recognized as a specific subclass. Neuraminidase inhibitors are a particular type of direct acting antiviral, representing a more focused group of agents that act directly to inhibit viral neuraminidase activity. This hierarchical relationship highlights that all neuraminidase inhibitors are direct acting antivirals, and all direct acting antivirals fall under the larger umbrella of systemic-use antivirals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza and الإنفلونزا are linked through a hierarchical linguistic relationship, with الإنفلونزا serving as the parent term for the English word influenza. This indicates that the Arabic designation الإنفلونزا represents the same viral infection described by the term influenza, clarifying cross‑language terminology and supporting accurate communication in medical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Zanamivir is classified as a neuraminidase inhibitor and direct‑acting antiviral, belonging to the group of neuraminidase inhibitors, direct‑acting antivirals. Influenza, which is also known by its Arabic name الإنفلونزا, is a viral respiratory illness that may be prevented by the use of zanamivir. This relationship highlights zanamivir’s role as a prophylactic and therapeutic agent against influenza.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1431
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza, referred to in Arabic as الالتهاب الرئوي, is a viral respiratory infection that can lead to or be associated with pneumonia. The broader term الالتهاب الرئوي also includes specific forms such as التهاب رئوي وليدي, which denotes neonatal pneumonia, a distinct subtype of pneumonia occurring in newborn infants. Understanding that influenza may precipitate pneumonia and that neonatal pneumonia is a particular manifestation of pneumonia aids clinicians in identifying at‑risk patients, especially infants, and in applying appropriate preventive and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 47
retrieved_docs.content: Within the respiratory disease hierarchy, influenza is inversely related to pulmonary inflammation, is a child condition of fungal‑type pneumonia, and serves as the parent condition of the broader syndrome termed “الإنفلونزا”. The neuraminidase inhibitor zanamivir, a direct‑acting antiviral, provides prophylaxis against “الإنفلونزا”. These relationships delineate the connections between specific antimicrobial and antiviral agents and their target respiratory infections, emphasize important drug‑drug interaction warnings, and clarify the hierarchical taxonomy of acute bronchitis, various pneumonia subtypes, and influenza, thereby supporting informed prescribing and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1144
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines that act as direct‑acting antivirals and neuraminidase inhibitors that act as direct‑acting antivirals are both subclasses of the broader category of direct‑acting antivirals. The antiviral drug rimantadine belongs to the cyclic‑amine direct‑acting antiviral class and exerts a physiologic effect characterized by alteration of cellular synthetic activity. Zanamivir belongs to the neuraminidase‑inhibitor direct‑acting antiviral class and can be used to prevent influenza infection. Influenza (الإنفلونزا) is a viral respiratory illness that may be complicated by bacterial pneumonia caused by cocci (التهاب رئوي بالعقديات). The antibiotic gemifloxacin is an appropriate treatment for this type of bacterial pneumonia. Thus, the knowledge links specific antiviral subclasses to their representative drugs, describes the mechanism of rimantadine, highlights zanamivir’s preventive role against influenza, and connects influenza to its potential bacterial pneumonia complication and its treatment with gemifloxacin.

retrieved_docs.content: therapies. Clinically, gemifloxacin may treat granulomatous pneumonia, a pulmonary complication that can follow influenza infection; zanamivir provides prophylaxis against influenza. Smallpox, an orthopoxvirus disease, can be treated with brincidofovir or tecovirimat. Together, these classifications, molecular targets, mechanisms of action, contraindications, and formulation details constitute an integrated framework for antiviral therapy, anticoagulation management, antifungal treatment, and peptide‑based diagnostic imaging in patient care.

The relationships also link drugs to preventive or therapeutic indications (HIV prevention, HIV treatment, hepatitis C, hepatitis B/D, influenza, smallpox, viral infection prophylaxis) and to epidemiologic contexts such as HIV infection (a viral infection associated with shared needle use) and influenza‑related pneumonia, thereby supporting informed therapeutic decision‑making and public‑health control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals are a subset of antivirals for systemic use. Within the direct‑acting antiviral category, two major subclasses are identified: cyclic amine direct‑acting antivirals and neuraminidase‑inhibitor direct‑acting antivirals. The drug rimantadine belongs to the cyclic amine direct‑acting antiviral class and is known to produce the physiologic effect of cellular synthetic activity alteration. Zanamivir is a member of the neuraminidase‑inhibitor direct‑acting antiviral class. This hierarchical organization clarifies how specific antiviral agents such as rimantadine and zanamivir fit into broader therapeutic categories and highlights the distinct mechanisms—alteration of cellular synthetic processes for rimantadine and neuraminidase inhibition for zanamivir—by which they exert antiviral activity.
